beats.text: Same product every time. But here's what we need to figure out: is this a coincidence, or does this always happen? And if it always happens... why?
That's three arrays now. Every time there were two expressions, same product. 15 and 15. 20 and 20. 21 and 21. Why do you think both expressions give the same product?
Why do both expressions give the same product?
options: Same dots — you just read them a different way
The numbers are small enough to match
Rows and columns always have the same number
It only works for some arrays

params.description: 3 × 5 = 15 highlights and pulses
5 × 3 = 15 highlights and pulses

beats.text: Here's what matters: when you flip an array, the dots don't change. 3 × 5 has the exact same dots as 5 × 3. You just read them differently. Same dots means same total. That is why both expressions give the same product. The answer is: same dots, you just read them a different way.
But can we prove it even more clearly? Watch this.

beats.text: And here's the important part. Did any dots disappear when I turned it? Did new dots show up?
No. Every single dot is still there. I just turned the array. Same dots, same total. That's why the product can't change.

params.description: First equivalence: 3 × 5 = 15
Turn-around fact: 5 × 3 = 15
Second equivalence: 3 × 6 = 18
Turn-around fact: 6 × 3 = 18
Third equivalence: 5 × 8 = 40
Turn-around fact: 8 × 5 = 40

beats.text: You proved something important. No matter which array you check, the two expressions always give the same product. This shows us that you can multiply the factors in any order and always get the same answer. These are turn-around facts.
Mathematicians have a name for this. They call it the commutative property of multiplication. But the idea is simple. You can change the order of the factors and get the same answer.
And here's why this is more than just interesting. It's a strategy, a plan for solving problems. If you know one fact, you already know its turn-around fact without doing any extra work.

Watch how the strategy works. If I know 4 times 6 is 24, do I need to compute 6 times 4? No, I can multiply in any order. Switch the factors. 6 times 4 is also 24. I didn't compute anything. I used what I already knew.
Your turn. You know 7 times 8 is 56. What's 8 times 7? Use the strategy.

validator.description: Student selected 56, correct, turn-around facts applied

beats.text: Right. 56. You didn't need to compute, you used the turn-around facts. You can multiply the factors in any order and get the same product.

validator.description: Student selected 15, added 7 and 8 instead of recognizing turn-around facts

beats.text: You added 7 and 8. We're multiplying. The turn-around facts tell us 8 times 7 equals the same as 7 times 8.

validator.description: Student selected 78, concatenated the digits instead of recognizing turn-around facts

beats.text: That's the two numbers written side by side. We need the product. When you switch the order of factors, the product stays the same.

validator.description: Student selected 54, recalled a different fact instead of recognizing turn-around facts

beats.text: Let's think about this together. We know 7 times 8 equals 56. The turn-around facts say we can switch the order. So 8 times 7 also equals 56.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

beats.text: Let me show you how this works. We know 7 times 8 equals 56. The turn-around facts tell us we can multiply in any order and get the same product. So 8 times 7 equals 56. We didn't compute anything new. We used what we already knew. The answer is 56.

Four dot arrays displayed as answer options. A) 4×6 | B) 8×3 | C) 6×4 | D) 2×12. All have 24 dots.

beats.text: Here's an array: 3 rows of 8. That's 24 dots. Now look at these four arrays. They ALL have 24 dots. But which one is the SAME array as 3 × 8, just rotated?
Which array is the same as 3 × 8, just rotated?

validator.description: Student selected B) 8 × 3, correct. Rotation swaps 3 rows of 8 into 8 rows of 3.

params.description: Option B highlights to confirm selection.
